facts.label: BLUE AND YELLOW
facts.story: Sweden's colors trace straight to the flag: an oft-quoted 1908 poem by Johan af Klercker cemented blue and yellow as national colors, and the football team has treated them as gospel ever since — yellow shirt, blue shorts, yellow socks, barely altered in over a century. The away kit simply flips the order: blue shirt, yellow shorts, blue socks. Even the women's national team carries the same identity, nicknamed Blågult — 'the blue-yellow' — a tribute to the same century-old scheme that never needed a redesign to stay recognizable.

facts.label: THE BRAZIL PROBLEM
facts.story: Brazil became Sweden's World Cup nemesis almost by accident. The two sides have met seven times in the tournament and Sweden has never won — five losses and two draws — including the 1958 final in Stockholm, where a 17-year-old named Pelé scored twice as Brazil beat the hosts 5–2 for their first title. Sweden also fell to Brazil in the 1950 group stage and the 1994 semi-final, a hoodoo now spanning five decades of near-misses against the same yellow-and-green shirt.

1934.headline: First Match, First Win

annotations.label: SILENCED IN BOLOGNA
annotations.story: Four days after the Argentina comeback, Sweden and Germany traded nothing for 81 minutes. Then Dunker struck, and under 1934's pure knockout format — no groups, no second leg — that single goal ended it. Sweden's debut tournament closed exactly as it opened: settled in the final ten minutes.

annotations.label: HANDMADE COLORS
annotations.story: No manufacturer's mark touched Sweden's 1934 shirt — just yellow wool and blue shorts cut to federation spec, with the three-crowns emblem stitched plain over the heart. Commercial branding was decades off; on the grainy newsreel from Bologna, the famous blue and yellow barely registered as more than light and shade.

annotations.label: A WEAKENED OPPONENT
annotations.story: Argentina showed up in Bologna missing most of the side that had lost the inaugural 1930 final — their clubs refused to release stars for a long ocean crossing, leaving a patched-together reserve XI. Sweden's first-ever World Cup victory came against opponents barely resembling the runners-up from four years earlier.

1934.story: Sweden's World Cup debut was the tournament's very first match, and they trailed before Sven Jonasson's brace and a goal from Kroon completed a 3–2 comeback over Argentina in Bologna. Four days later, in the era's single-elimination format, a Dunker goal wasn't enough — Germany won 2–1, ending Sweden's tournament in the quarter-final.
1934.homeColors: yellow shirt, blue shorts, yellow socks

1938.headline: Eight Goals, Then a Collapse
1938.story: Austria's forced withdrawal after the Anschluss gave Sweden a bye into the quarter-finals, where they demolished Cuba 8–0 — still their biggest World Cup win, built on hat-tricks from Harry Andersson and Gustav Wetterström. Hungary ended the run 5–1 in the semi-final, and a tired Sweden lost the third-place match to Brazil 4–2.
1938.homeColors: yellow shirt, blue shorts, yellow socks

matches.scorers: Andersson 9', Wetterström 22', Wetterström 37', Wetterström 44', Keller 80', Andersson 81', Nyberg 84', Andersson 89'

1950.headline: Bronze Built on Survival
1950.story: Sweden beat Italy and drew Paraguay to reach the four-team final pool, then were routed 7–1 by Brazil in Rio — still their heaviest World Cup defeat — before falling to Uruguay and closing with a 3–1 win over Spain. It was enough for third place, a bronze finish built more on survival than form.
1950.homeColors: yellow shirt, blue shorts, yellow socks

1958.headline: So Close, On Home Soil
1958.story: Hosting the tournament, Sweden beat Hungary, drew Wales, and eliminated the Soviet Union before stunning defending champions West Germany 3–1 in the semi-final at Råsunda. In the final, a 17-year-old Pelé scored twice as Brazil won 5–2 in Stockholm — the best finish in Swedish football history, delivered in front of a home crowd.
1958.homeColors: yellow shirt, blue shorts, yellow socks (unbranded, hosts' kit)
1958.awayColors: blue shirt, yellow shorts, blue socks

1970.headline: One Win, One Round
1970.story: An opening 1–0 loss to Italy put Sweden behind before they'd started. Tom Turesson's equalizer earned a point against Israel, and Ove Grahn's late goal beat Uruguay 1–0, but it wasn't enough to escape a tight Group 2, and Sweden went out after the first round.
1970.homeColors: yellow shirt, blue shorts, yellow socks
1970.awayColors: blue shirt, yellow shorts, blue socks

1974.headline: Edström's Golden Run
1974.story: Ralf Edström scored four goals across the tournament, including two in a 3–0 win over Uruguay that carried Sweden through their first group unbeaten. In the second-round group, hosts West Germany survived a Swedish fightback to win 4–2, and despite beating Yugoslavia, Sweden's run ended one round short of the semi-finals.
1974.homeColors: yellow shirt, blue shorts, yellow socks (Adidas)
1974.awayColors: blue shirt, yellow shorts, blue socks

1978.headline: A Draw, Then Silence
1978.story: Sjöberg's equalizer salvaged a 1–1 draw with eventual bronze medalists Brazil in the opener, but back-to-back 1–0 losses to Austria and Spain sent Sweden home without a win — a quiet World Cup that did little to build on the promise of the opening result.
1978.homeColors: yellow shirt, blue shorts, yellow socks (Adidas)
1978.awayColors: blue shirt, yellow shorts, blue socks

1990.headline: Winless in Italy
1990.story: Tomas Brolin's strike against Brazil gave brief hope, but Sweden lost all three group games — including a shock defeat to Costa Rica — to finish without a point. It remains the low point of the team's World Cup history, a group-stage sweep with nothing to show for it.
1990.homeColors: yellow shirt, blue shorts, yellow socks (Adidas)
1990.awayColors: blue shirt, yellow shorts, blue socks

1994.headline: Andersson's Summer in the Sun
1994.story: Kennet Andersson scored five goals across the tournament, sharing the Bronze Boot, as Sweden survived a penalty shootout over Romania before eventual champions Brazil edged them 1–0 in the semi-final. Goals from Brolin, Mild, and a young Henrik Larsson crushed Bulgaria 4–0 in the third-place match, sealing Sweden's best finish since 1958.
1994.homeColors: yellow shirt, blue shorts, yellow socks (Adidas)
1994.awayColors: blue shirt, yellow shorts, blue socks

2002.headline: Golden Goal Heartbreak
2002.story: Anders Svensson's late equalizer salvaged a point against Argentina, and Henrik Larsson's brace beat Nigeria as Sweden advanced from a loaded group. Senegal ended the run in the Round of 16, snatching a golden-goal winner in extra time — one of the tournament's biggest upsets, and Sweden's earliest exit of the decade.
2002.homeColors: yellow shirt, blue shorts, yellow socks (Adidas)
2002.awayColors: blue shirt, yellow shorts, blue socks

2006.headline: Peak in the Group
2006.story: Freddie Ljungberg's goal beat Paraguay, and a spirited 2–2 draw with England kept Sweden in contention, but the group stage proved to be the peak. Hosts Germany eliminated them 2–0 in the Round of 16, a fourth straight World Cup ending without reaching the quarter-finals.
2006.homeColors: yellow shirt, blue shorts, yellow socks (Umbro)
2006.awayColors: blue shirt, yellow shorts, blue socks

2018.headline: Forsberg's Strike, Then England
2018.story: Emil Forsberg's second-half strike beat Switzerland in the Round of 16, sending Sweden to their first World Cup quarter-final since 1994. Ola Toivonen's outrageous chip had rattled Germany in the group stage, and wins over South Korea and Mexico carried the run — until England ended it 2–0 in the quarter-final.
2018.homeColors: yellow shirt, blue shorts, yellow socks (Adidas)
2018.awayColors: blue shirt, yellow shorts, blue socks
